sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a transparent vascular model with adjustable stenosis used to measure fluid speed and downstream pressure at constant pump output to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that for an incompressible fluid, narrowing a tube increases speed at the narrow segment and can lower local static pressure when height changes are negligible. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as A1v1 = A2v2 and P + 1/2ρv^2 + ρgh = constant along a streamline. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved decreasing the cross-sectional area of the constricted segment while maintaining the same volumetric flow rate. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: velocity increased at the narrowed segment and the measured static pressure there decreased. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that fluid density, pump flow rate, tube height, and pressure sensor calibration were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that narrowing the tube must lower velocity because there is less room for fluid to move. Another was that static pressure and flow speed always increase together in a horizontal streamline. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to vascular stenosis model, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the vascular stenosis model experiment?

choices.A: Narrowing the tube must lower velocity because there is less room for fluid to move
choices.B: Static pressure and flow speed always increase together in a horizontal streamline, even though the passage states that fluid density was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.C: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls
choices.D: The pattern is best explained by continuity increasing speed through the narrowed region with Bernoulli reasoning predicting lower local static pressure

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Narrowing the tube must lower velocity because there is less room for fluid to move conflicts with the stated relationship, A1v1 = A2v2 and P + 1/2ρv^2 + ρgh = constant along a streamline, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that velocity increased at the narrowed segment and the measured static pressure there decreased and states that fluid density, pump flow rate, tube height, and pressure sensor calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Static pressure and flow speed always increase together in a horizontal streamline, even though the passage states that fluid density was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure conflicts with the stated relationship, A1v1 = A2v2 and P + 1/2ρv^2 + ρgh = constant along a streamline, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that velocity increased at the narrowed segment and the measured static pressure there decreased and states that fluid density, pump flow rate, tube height, and pressure sensor calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, A1v1 = A2v2 and P + 1/2ρv^2 + ρgh = constant along a streamline, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that velocity increased at the narrowed segment and the measured static pressure there decreased and states that fluid density, pump flow rate, tube height, and pressure sensor calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as A1v1 = A2v2 and P + 1/2ρv^2 + ρgh = constant along a streamline, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, velocity increased at the narrowed segment and the measured static pressure there decreased, while fluid density, pump flow rate, tube height, and pressure sensor calibration were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The pattern is best explained by continuity increasing speed through the narrowed region with Bernoulli reasoning predicting lower local static pressure. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.

questions.stem: Which follow-up prediction is most consistent with the same C/P reasoning?

choices.A: Changing only the unit label would change the underlying physical result, even though the passage states that fluid density was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.B: The same numerical response should occur even if the manipulated variable is reversed
choices.C: The relationship would not apply because the system was modeled in a biological or clinical context

questions.topic: Continuity and pressure in a narrowed vessel

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Fluid motion in vessels depends on area, speed, pressure, and energy conservation along streamlines
choices.B: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that fluid density was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure

questions.topic: Continuity and pressure in a narrowed vessel

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated cells suspended in density media to predict whether they float, sink, or remain suspended during a centrifuge-free separation step to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid, so the net direction depends on object density relative to fluid density. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as Fb = ρfluid Vg and weight = ρobject Vg. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved placing identical cell-sized beads of different density into media with increasing density. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: beads floated only when the medium density exceeded bead density and sank when bead density was greater. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that bead radius, temperature, medium viscosity, and observation time were held constant. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that larger objects always float better regardless of their density relative to the fluid. Another was that centrifugation is required for buoyant force to exist in a liquid. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to cell-density flotation assay, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the cell-density flotation assay experiment?

choices.A: Larger objects always float better regardless of their density relative to the fluid
choices.B: Centrifugation is required for buoyant force to exist in a liquid
choices.C: The result is best explained by comparing the bead density with the density of displaced medium
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Larger objects always float better regardless of their density relative to the fluid conflicts with the stated relationship, Fb = ρfluid Vg and weight = ρobject Vg, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that beads floated only when the medium density exceeded bead density and sank when bead density was greater and states that bead radius, temperature, medium viscosity, and observation time were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
